content: Antibiotic overprescription is a major public health threat: 30-50% of antibiotic use in humans is unnecessary or inappropriate. Common scenarios: viral respiratory infections (colds, flu, bronchitis) where antibiotics have no benefit; unnecessarily broad-spectrum agents when narrow would suffice; excessive duration (7 days when 3-5 adequate); agricultural use (70% of US antibiotics sold for livestock). Consequences include: Clostridioides difficile infection (500,000 cases/year US, 29,000 deaths), adverse drug reactions, microbiome disruption linked to obesity/allergies, and antimicrobial resistance (AMR). AMR causes 2.8 million infections and 35,000 deaths annually in the US, projected to cause 10 million deaths globally by 2050 without intervention.

Antibiotic stewardship interventions that work: (1) Audit and feedback - prospective review with prescriber feedback reduces inappropriate use 20-40%; (2) Clinical decision support - EHR alerts for duplicate therapy, renal dosing, culture follow-up; (3) Delayed prescribing - 'wait-and-see' prescriptions for otitis media, sinusitis reducing fill rates 50-70%; (4) Diagnostic stewardship - procalcitonin testing to guide antibiotic discontinuation in respiratory infections (reduces duration 2-3 days); (5) Public education - campaigns like CDC's 'Be Antibiotics Aware' reducing patient expectations; (6) Accountability - designating stewardship leaders, tracking/prescribing metrics. Outpatient stewardship reduced antibiotic prescribing for acute respiratory infections from 50% to 35% over 10 years. Inpatient programs show 20-30% reduction in antibiotic use without worse outcomes.

Measuring appropriate antibiotic use: (1) Outpatient metrics - % of URI visits with antibiotics (target <35%), % of strep pharyngitis with testing before prescribing (target >90%); (2) Inpatient metrics - days of therapy per 1,000 patient-days, % compliance with surgical prophylaxis guidelines (stop within 24 hours); (3) Resistance surveillance - CDC's AR Isolate Bank tracks resistance patterns; (4) Peer comparison - facility-level prescribing compared to regional/national benchmarks. High-performing systems achieve: <25% antibiotic prescribing for viral URIs, >95% guideline-concordant surgical prophylaxis, <400 days of therapy/1,000 patient-days. Regional variation persists: some US states have 2x higher prescribing rates than others despite similar disease burden, suggesting practice pattern rather than patient need drives variation.
